questions.question: What is the primary purpose of self-care during grief, according to this lesson?

options.text: To optimize your health and productivity during a difficult time
To speed up the grieving process so you recover faster
To keep your body stable enough to grieve and prevent physical depletion
To return to your normal routine as quickly as possible

questions.explanation: Self-care during grief serves a simple purpose: keeping your body stable enough to grieve, and preventing the kind of physical depletion that makes everything harder. It is not about improvement, productivity, or getting better faster — it is about survival with compassion.

questions.question: What is 'pacing' as described in this lesson?

options.text: Walking at a specific speed as a mindfulness exercise
Pushing through tasks to maintain your normal productivity
Alternating between small efforts and intentional rest, rather than pushing through
Setting a strict daily schedule and sticking to it no matter what

questions.explanation: Pacing is a concept from chronic illness management that applies well to grief: doing a little, then resting. Not pushing through. Not expecting consistent productivity. Just alternating between small efforts and intentional rest. It protects you from the crash that follows pushing too hard.

questions.question: What does self-compassion during grief look like?

options.text: Telling yourself to stay positive and focus on the good things
Comparing your grief to others to gain perspective
Pushing through pain to prove you are strong
Treating yourself with the kindness you would offer a dear friend in the same situation

questions.explanation: Self-compassion during grief means treating yourself with the kindness you would offer a dear friend going through the same thing. Research consistently links self-compassion during bereavement with better adjustment and less self-criticism. It is not a luxury — it is a foundation.

questions.question: Why does the lesson warn about using alcohol and other substances to cope with grief?

options.text: Because all substance use is morally wrong
Because substances disrupt sleep, worsen mood over time, delay grief processing, and can become habitual
Because they make grief waves more intense when they do occur
Because healthcare providers will not treat you if you use substances

questions.explanation: While using substances to numb pain is understandable, they disrupt sleep quality, worsen mood over time, delay grief processing, and can quickly become habitual when paired with ongoing emotional pain. The lesson encourages honesty with yourself and seeking support if you notice increasing use.

questions.question: What does the lesson say about isolation during grief?

options.text: All alone time is harmful and should be avoided completely
Some solitude is healthy, but extended isolation can deepen depression and make grief harder to process
You should always be surrounded by others to properly grieve
Isolation is the best strategy until you feel ready to be social

questions.explanation: Some solitude during grief is healthy and needed. But extended isolation — weeks of avoiding all human contact — can deepen depression and make grief harder to process. Even brief, low-pressure contact like a short phone call or sitting in the same room as another person can help.
